content: Maximum drawdown measures the largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value, expressed as a percentage. Unlike volatility which treats upside and downside equally, max drawdown captures actual worst-case loss an investor would have experienced. A 30% max drawdown means at some point the portfolio lost 30% from its previous high before recovering. Drawdown duration matters too—some strategies recover quickly, others take years. Calmar ratio (annual return / max drawdown) compares returns to worst-case pain. Hedge funds often target max drawdowns under 15%; equity indices can experience 50%+ drawdowns in severe bear markets (S&P 500 fell 57% in 2008-09). Investors should consider whether they can emotionally withstand a strategy's historical max drawdown.
